name: Roger Marshall
education: Butler Community College (Associate's, 1980); Kansas State University (B.S. Biochemistry, 1982); University of Kansas School of Medicine (M.D., 1987)
family: Married to Laina Marshall (40+ years); four children (Lauren, Victor, Matt, Cal); four grandchildren
whoTheyAre:
Roger Marshall grew up on a fifth-generation Kansas farm in Butler County, near El Dorado. He was the first person in his family to go to college. After earning a biochemistry degree from Kansas State and a medical degree from the University of Kansas, he completed his residency in obstetrics and gynecology at Bayfront Medical Center in St. Petersburg, Florida. He also served seven years in the U.S. Army Reserve, reaching the rank of Captain. In 1991, he and his wife Laina moved to Great Bend, Kansas, where he would spend the next 25 years delivering babies and building a life.

Over those decades in Great Bend, Marshall delivered more than 5,000 babies. He became chairman of the board at Great Bend Regional Hospital, a role he held for 14 years. He served as vice chairman of the board at Farmers Bank and Trust, became a Rotary International district governor, and was deeply involved in his church and local civic organizations. He and Laina raised four children and have been married for more than 40 years.

Marshall entered politics in 2016, when he ran against three-term incumbent congressman Tim Huelskamp in the Republican primary for Kansas's 1st Congressional District. Huelskamp had lost his seat on the House Agriculture Committee, angering Kansas farm groups who then backed Marshall. He won the primary convincingly and went on to serve two terms in the U.S. House. In 2020, he ran for the U.S. Senate and won with 53.2% of the vote. He is now running for re-election in 2026.
theirRecord:
In the Senate, Marshall serves on the Agriculture, Finance, Health/Education/Labor/Pensions (HELP), and Budget committees. He chairs the Agriculture subcommittee on Conservation and the HELP subcommittee on Primary Health. His first bill signed into law was the Ensuring Innovation Act, a bipartisan measure that closed loopholes drug companies used to block cheaper generic medications. He has introduced dozens of bills focused on healthcare, agriculture, firearms policy, and border security.

Marshall has a 100% pro-life voting record and made pro-life legislation his first priority as a senator. He voted with President Trump's positions 98% of the time, making him one of the most aligned senators in Congress. On January 6, 2021, he was one of only eight Republican senators to vote against certifying the 2020 presidential election results in both Arizona and Pennsylvania. He later said it was "a decision from my heart" based on concerns that some states broke their own election laws, but added he was "so ready to move on." He also voted against creating an independent commission to investigate the January 6 Capitol attack.

Marshall drew national attention in January 2022 when Dr. Anthony Fauci was caught on a hot mic calling him "a moron" during a Senate hearing about COVID financial disclosures. Marshall responded by introducing the FAUCI Act to require public posting of top officials' financial records. In March 2025, he left a town hall in Oakley, Kansas, roughly 20 minutes early after constituents confronted him about federal budget cuts. His office later claimed "paid Democratic operatives" had disrupted the event, a claim local attendees strongly denied. He voted against the bipartisan gun safety bill passed after the Uvalde school shooting, calling red flag provisions a "gun-grabbing scheme." He also voted against a $95 billion foreign aid package for Ukraine and Israel, breaking with Kansas colleague Senator Jerry Moran.
whatYouShouldKnow: Marshall is a fifth-generation Kansas farmer and delivered more than 5,000 babies as an OB-GYN in Great Bend over 25 years before entering politics.
He was one of only eight Republican senators to vote against certifying the 2020 presidential election on January 6, 2021, objecting to results in both Arizona and Pennsylvania.

He left a town hall in Oakley, Kansas in March 2025 about 20 minutes early after being confronted by constituents about federal budget cuts. His office later called attendees 'paid Democratic operatives,' which local residents denied.

He owns a $1.2 million home in Sarasota, Florida, with a boat dock on a bayou, in addition to a 1,120-square-foot cabin in western Kansas that he lists as his primary residence. Politico reported he charged taxpayers more than $4,900 for seven trips to Sarasota in 2021.
He has a 100% pro-life voting record and opposes abortion even in cases of rape and incest. His first bill as a senator was pro-life legislation.

As a doctor, he supported COVID vaccines as effective but strongly opposed all vaccine mandates. He personally recommended ivermectin for COVID, had a Facebook post removed for misinformation, and said he was 'not convinced the masks really work at all.'
Donald Trump gave Marshall his 'Complete and Total Endorsement' for re-election in July 2025. Marshall has voted with Trump's positions 98% of the time.
He has been deeply involved at First Christian Church in Great Bend for over 25 years, serving as elder, deacon, board chairman, and Sunday school teacher.
whereTheyWorship:
Roger Marshall has been a member of First Christian Church in Great Bend, Kansas, for more than 25 years. The church is part of the Christian Church (Disciples of Christ) denomination. Marshall has not just attended services -- he has held virtually every leadership role the church offers. He has served as an elder, deacon, and board chairman. He and his wife Laina taught Sunday school together for over 25 years.

Marshall says he accepted Jesus Christ as his Lord and Savior at a Fellowship of Christian Athletes event in high school and has tried to read the Bible every day since he was 10 years old. He has referenced his faith in Senate floor speeches and connects his pro-life stance directly to both his Christian beliefs and his career delivering thousands of babies. Among all officials investigated for this project, Marshall has one of the deepest documented records of sustained church leadership.

church.name: First Christian Church, Great Bend
church.details: Marshall has served as elder, deacon, board chairman, and Sunday school teacher (25+ years) at this congregation.

quotes.text: I don't wear my faith on my shirt sleeve, but in my heart.
I have tried to read the Bible every day since I was 10 years of age, so a lot of the wisdom I've been given comes from reading the Bible.
Calling me a moron during a Senate hearing may have alleviated the stress of the least trusted bureaucrat in America, but it didn't take away from the facts. We need the truth Dr. Fauci!
I was concerned then and I still am today that six states broke their own laws or their own constitution, but it's time to move on.
As an OBGYN who delivered more than 5,000 babies, I remain as dedicated as ever to doing everything in my power as a U.S. Senator to protect life at all costs.
Every decision I make, I always have to be true to my faith.

campaignFinance.narrative: Roger Marshall runs one of the best-funded Senate campaigns in Kansas history. By the end of 2025, his committee -- Kansans for Marshall -- had roughly $4.07 million cash on hand, with about $621,400 raised in the fourth quarter of 2025 alone. His 2020 Senate race drew massive outside support, including $1.9 million in independent expenditures from the U.S. Chamber of Commerce. His top corporate donors include Koch Industries and Spirit AeroSystems, both of which gave the maximum $10,000 through their PACs during his 2020 run. Agriculture and health/medical industries are major donor categories, reflecting his background as a Kansas farmer's son and a physician. After Marshall voted to object to the 2020 election certification on January 6, some major donors publicly distanced themselves, though the long-term impact on his fundraising appears limited given his current war chest.
campaignFinance.undisclosed: Koch Industries was the biggest overall corporate donor to GOP spending groups ($13.75 million total across all candidates) and has deep Kansas ties. Detailed 2026-cycle donor breakdowns are available at OpenSecrets and FEC.gov.
